data.title: Largest U.S. Banks Lead Sector Market Performance In September
data.description:
Although most of the US banking sector traded down in September, the Big Four banks bucked the trend, propping up the market-cap weighted index.

Each of the four US banks with more than $1 trillion in total assets as of June 30 posted positive total returns in September. Citigroup Inc. (C) led the way, trading up 5.1%. JPMorgan Chase & Co. (JPM), Bank of America Corp. (BAC) and Wells Fargo & Co. (WFC) followed with returns of 4.6%, 2.2% and 2.0%, respectively.

Those positive returns did not extend to the next largest asset tier. Eight of the 10 banks with assets between $100 billion and $1 trillion recorded negative returns; Citizens Financial Group Inc. (CFG) traded up 1.7% and U.S. Bancorp (USB) ticked up a fraction of a percent.

Banks between $50 billion and $100 billion in assets fared a bit better. Six of the 19 stocks went up in September, highlighted by Valley National Bancorp's (VLY) 2.4% return. Of the remaining 175 banks in an S&P Global Market Intelligence analysis, 16 experienced a positive total return in September.

For all 208 banks in the analysis, the median return in September was negative 3.4%, which trailed the 1.6% return of the S&P US BMI Banks index and the 3.7% return of the S&P 500. Market pressure pushed down valuations. The median price-to-adjusted tangible book value (TBV) for the industry was 141.9% as of Sept. 30, down from 145.4% at the end of August.

Least expensive banks

Dallas-based First Foundation Inc. (FFWM) was the least expensive bank in the analysis by price-to-adjusted TBV for the ninth month in a row. Its valuation was 50.5% at the end of September and would have been higher if preferred shares from a capital raise in July 2024 were converted to common stock. Such a conversion would have dropped the bank's basic TBV to $9.34 per common share from $11.65 per common share as of June 30, according to a July 31 filing.

First Internet Bancorp (INBK), which had the second-lowest price-to-adjusted TBV, was the fourth-weakest market performer in the analysis in September, with a total return of negative 10.5%. The bank expects to report a material impairment charge in the third quarter from the sale of performing single-tenant lease financing loans in September.

The No. 15 bank, OceanFirst Financial Corp. (OCFC), plans to pursue organic growth rather than M&A. The Toms River, New Jersey-based bank has been hiring bankers who can attract low-cost deposits.

Most expensive banks

The Bancorp Inc. (TBBK) screened as the highest-valued bank for the third consecutive month. Its price-to-adjusted TBV was 438.5% as of Sept. 30, down 8 percentage points from Aug. 29, but more than 100 percentage points higher than any other bank in the analysis.

The second-most expensive bank — Dewitt, New York-based Community Financial System Inc. (CBU) — announced a minority investment in Leap Holdings Inc. in September.

Triumph Financial Inc. (TFIN), the No. 8 bank by highest valuation, disclosed its exposure to subprime auto lender Tricolor Holdings LLC, which filed for Chapter 7 bankruptcy on Sept. 10. Triumph had the worst monthly return in the analysis at negative 18.6%.

Also in September, Nicolet Bankshares Inc. (NIC), the 18th-most expensive bank, announced leadership continuity through 2030. The Green Bay, Wisconsin-based bank has earned a return on average tangible common equity in excess of 17% for the last seven quarters.

About this analysis

S&P Global Market Intelligence analyzed US banks trading on the Nasdaq, NYSE or NYSE American with total assets of more than $3 billion. The analysis excludes banks in the mutual holding company ownership structure and other operating subsidiaries.

Adjusted tangible book value is calculated as the sum of tangible common equity, loss reserves and unrealized gain or loss from held-to-maturity securities, tax-adjusted at the 21% corporate rate, less nonperforming assets and loans 90 or more days past due but still accruing interest, divided by common shares outstanding.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: Weekly Indicators: Almost Completely Unaffected By Government Shutdown
data.description:
Purpose

I look at the high-frequency weekly indicators because while they can be very noisy, they provide a good nowcast of the economy, and will telegraph the maintenance or change in the economy well before monthly or quarterly data is available. They are also an excellent way to "mark your beliefs to market." In general, I go in order of long leading indicators, then short leading indicators, then coincident indicators.

A Note on Methodology

Data is presented in a "just the facts, ma'am" format with a minimum of commentary so that bias is minimized.

Where relevant, I include 12-month highs and lows in the data in parentheses to the right. All data taken from St. Louis FRED unless otherwise linked.

A few items (e.g., Financial Conditions indexes, regional Fed indexes, stock prices, the yield curve) have their own metrics based on long-term studies of their behavior.

Where data is seasonally adjusted, generally it is scored positively if it is within the top 1/3 of that range, negative in the bottom 1/3, and neutral in between. Where it is not seasonally adjusted, and there are seasonal issues, waiting for the YoY change to change sign will lag the turning point. Thus I make use of a convention: data is scored neutral if it is less than 1/2 as positive/negative as at its 12-month extreme.

With long leading indicators, which by definition turn at least 12 months before a turning point in the economy as a whole, there is an additional rule: data is automatically negative if, during an expansion, it has not made a new peak in the past year, with the sole exception that it is scored neutral if it is moving in the right direction and is close to making a new high.

For all series where a graph is available, I have provided a link to where the relevant graph can be found.

Recap of Monthly Reports

September data included contractionary readings in the ISM manufacturing index as well as its new orders and employment indexes, while the ISM services index was exactly flat, while its new orders was slightly expansionary, but its employment index was also contractionary.

August data included manufacturers new durable goods orders, core capital goods orders, and consumer durable goods new orders, all of which increased. House prices as measured by both the Case Shiller and FHFA Indexes decreased again. JOLTS data included an increase in openings, but declines in both hires and quits, and an increase in layoffs.

Long leading indicators

Interest rates and credit spreads

Rates

  • BAA corporate bond index 5.78%, down -0.07% w/w (1-yr range: 5.47-6.43)
  • 10-year Treasury bonds 4.12%, down -0.06% w/w (3.66-4.98)
  • Credit spread 1.66%, down -0.01% w/w (1.36-2.02)

(Graph at Moody's Seasoned Baa Corporate Bond Yield | FRED | St. Louis Fed )

Yield curve

  • 10 year minus 2 year: +0.55%, up +0.02% w/w (-1.07 - +0.55) (new 12 month high)
  • 10 year minus 3 month: +0.16%, down -0.06% w/w (-1.65 - 0.17)
  • 2 year minus Fed funds: -0.52%, down -0.08% w/w 3.56-4.33

(Graph at 10-Year Treasury Constant Maturity Minus 2-Year Treasury Constant Maturity | FRED | St. Louis Fed )

30-Year conventional mortgage rate (from Mortgage News Daily)

  • 6.34%, down -.04% w/w (6.13-7.26)

Treasury and corporate bond yields, as well as mortgage rates, remain lower than their near 12 month highs of a number of months ago, and declined further after the poor August jobs report. The spreads between Treasuries and investment grade and high yield bonds continue to show little change, having meandered in a relatively narrow range for most of the past several years.

The yield curve steepened between short and long rates after the Fed decision, but otherwise remains where it has been, more or less, for months, with the 10-2 year spread being the most positive, and the 2 year vs. Fed funds being the most negative. The 10 year minus 3 month retreated to neutral this week. It is particularly interesting that the 2 year vs. Fed funds spread continues to be very negative.

Housing

Mortgage applications (from the Mortgage Bankers Association)

  • Purchase apps down -1% to 161 (114-163) [SA]
  • Purchase apps 4 wk avg. up +4 to 161 [SA]
  • Purchase apps YoY% up +16% [NSA]
  • Purchase apps YoY 4 wk avg. +19% [NSA]
  • Refi apps down -21% w/w [SA]
  • Refi apps YoY up +17% [SA]

*[SA] = seasonally adjusted, [NSA] = not seasonally adjusted

Real Estate Loans (from the FRB)

  • Up less than +0.1% w/w
  • Up +1.4% YoY

(Graph at Real Estate Loans, All Commercial Banks | FRED | St. Louis Fed )

Both purchase mortgage and refinancing applications have been volatile, but within a limited range over the past 18 months. Both are now substantially higher YoY, but remain close to their longer term bottoms, so they generally score neutral.

In 2023 real estate loans sank below 1/2 of their 12 month high, the last housing indicator to turn negative, and have generally continued to worsen. This week they made yet another multi-year low.

Money supply

The Federal Reserve has discontinued this weekly series. Data is now only released monthly. August data was just released last week:

  • M1 m/m up +0.2%, YoY Real M1 up +1.5% ( -1.4% 8/23 - +1.8% 7/25)
  • M2 m/m up +0.4%, YoY Real M2 up +1.8% (2.1% June 2025 3 year high) (worst -8.6% 9/23)

No recession has happened without a YoY real M1 negative, or YoY real M2 below +2.5%. Real M2 fell below that threshold in March 2022. Real M1 also turned negative as of May 2022. In the past, when real money supply has rebounded to the levels it is at now, it has either been during the latter part of a recession, or in the period of recovery, so both of these are positive long leading metrics now.

Corporate profits (Q2 actual, Q3 estimated) from I/B/E/S via FactSet at p. 33)

  • Q2 unchanged at 67.03, up +5.3% q/q
  • Q3 down -0.07 to 67.46, up +0.6% q/q

FactSet estimates earnings, which are replaced by actual earnings as they are reported, and are updated weekly. The "neutral" band is +/-3%. I also average the previous two quarters together, until at least 100 companies have actually reported. In Q1 earnings wound up lower by more than -3%, which turned the rating negative. But in Q2 earnings rebounded sharply, causing the rating change to positive.

Credit conditions (from the Chicago Fed)

  • Financial Conditions Index up +.01 ( looser ) to -0.56 (-0.42 - -0.67)
  • Adjusted Index (removing background economic conditions) up +.01 (looser) to -0.52(-0.34 - -0.61)
  • Leverage subindex up +0.05 (less loose) to -0.28 (+0.13 - -0.76)

In these indexes, lower = better for the economy. The Chicago Fed's Adjusted Index's real break-even point is roughly -0.25. In the leverage index, a negative number is good, a positive poor. The historical breakeven point has been -0.5 for the unadjusted Index. After briefly deteriorating to neutral several months ago, all three had been solidly positive, but the unadjusted index remains neutral.

Short leading indicators

Economic Indicators from the late Jeff Miller’s “Weighing the Week Ahead”

  • Miller Score (formerly "C-Score"): up +13 to 380, up +3 m/m (288 9/27/24 - 421 7/25/25) Components: Yield curve 64 Phillips curve 316
  • St. Louis Fed Financial Stress Index: up +0.00567 to -0.5340 (+0.5569 4/11/25 - -.9751 1/24/25)
  • BCIp from Georg Vrba: 57.5 as of 9/11/25 iM's Business Cycle Index (100 is max value, below 25 is recession signal averaging 20 weeks ahead)

The Miller Score is designed to look 52 weeks ahead for whether or not a recession is possible. Any score over 500 means no recession. This number fell below that threshold at the beginning of August 2021, so not only is it negative, but we are now years into the “recession eligible” time period. This indicator is heavily influenced by both the yield curve and the still very low unemployment rate vs. gas prices (expecting a reversal) which explains why it remains negative. My colleague who compiles this score began disaggregating the two components several months ago. Gas prices and yield curve gyrations remain the most volatile inputs.

The St. Louis Financial Stress index is one where a negative score is a positive for the economy, and during its limited existence, has risen above zero before a recession by less than one year. It remained very positive until briefly in April. It then recovered, and until last week remained among its better post-pandemic readings.

For 18 months, IM has stated that their system does not forecast a recession. Recently it did edge closer to their threshold, but has since rebounded.

Trade-weighted US$

  • Up +0.64 to 120.86 w/w, down -0.4% YoY (last week) (broad) (119.67 - 130.21) (Graph at Nominal Broad U.S. Dollar Index
  • Down -0.45 to 97.71 w/w, down -4.7% YoY (major currencies) (graph at link) (96.22- 110.18)

The neutral range for the US$ is between unchanged to 5% higher YoY. At 5% higher or more YoY, the strength suppresses exports and incentivizes imports, a negative for the economy. The trend in these measures had been from below 0 (thus positive) to neutral, to above 5% (negative) for 18 months, until the beginning of this year. Both measures are lower YoY now, so are positives.

I mechanically score a slowly depreciating US$ as a positive because it makes exports cheaper and imports more expensive, thereby assisting domestic production. If it is more a sign of relative US weakness - and given the behavior of precious metals, I suspect it is - then it would warrant a different conclusion.

Commodity prices

Bloomberg Commodity Index

  • Up +0.25 to 105.35 (95.97 - 107.71)
  • Up +3.2% YoY

(Graph at https://www.marketwatch.com/investing/index/bcom?countrycode=xx )

Bloomberg Industrial metals ETF (from Bloomberg) (graph at link)

  • 151.54, up +6.23 w/w (130.47 -160.83 )
  • Down -4.4% YoY

This spring the industrial measure gradually weakened from positive to negative. Then the broad commodity index declined sharply into negative territory, but then rebounded into neutral range and now positive. By contrast, the industrial metals measure remained very negative. This can be attributed to whether or not oil is included among the commodities tracked. Last week the industrial measure turned back higher YoY, and so positive. Please note, importantly, that if commodities were not priced in US$ terms, but in other major currencies, it is a completely different story. In particular, that industrial commodities are weaker YoY, despite a weaker US$, is definitely not a sign of global strength. Additionally, BCOM includes precious metals (gold and silver), without which it would be neutral, and in non-US$ terms is sharply negative.

Stock prices S&P 500 (from CNBC) (graph at link)

  • Up +1.1% to 6715.79

Since July stocks have made repeated new all-time highs. As a result, this indicator is positive.

Regional Fed New Orders Indexes

(*indicates report this week)

  • Empire State https://www.newyorkfed.org/survey/empire/empiresurvey_overview.html up +4.5 to +19.9
  • Philly up +14.3 to +12.4
  • Richmond down -9 to -15
  • Kansas City down -3 to +2
  • *Dallas down -8.4 to -2.6
  • Month-over-month rolling average: down -2 to +3

The regional average is more volatile than the ISM manufacturing index, but usually correctly forecasts its month-over-month direction. Since spring 2022, these gradually declined to neutral and then negative. In the past two months, these have improved to neutral and now to positive. In September they have softened somewhat.

Since May I have added the regional service indexes. Those reports posted in the past week noted with an *:

  • Empire State down -7.7 to -19.4
  • Philly up +14.4 to +24.6
  • Richmond down -13 to -7
  • Kansas City down -13 to -9
  • *Dallas down -12.4 to -5.6
  • Month over month rolling average: down -3 to -3

The most positive recent trend in all of the high frequency data in August was the sharp rebound in manufacturing all the way to solidly positive. Meanwhile the regional non-manufacturing reports, which had stabilized at negative levels since May, rebounded all the way to neutral, and two weeks ago to positive. Since then they have retreated back to negative.

Employment metrics

Initial jobless claims (NOT AVAILABLE DUE TO GOVERNMENT SHUTDOWN)

  • 218,000, down -14,000 w/w, down -1.4% YoY (last week)
  • 4-week average 237,500, down -2,750 w/w, up 5.4% YoY (last week)

(Graph at St. Louis FRED)

Jobless claims were positive for most of 2024, except for hurricane-influenced weeks during the autumn. Then they turned moderately negative through midyear this year. But in July and August this indicator improved to positive before retreating in September.

This metric was reported during the last several government shutdowns, perhaps because it simply involved adding up all the States’ reports and then applying a seasonal factor. Many States do report their own individual numbers, but these were not available in time for this week’s report.

Temporary staffing index (from the American Staffing Association) (graph at link) *****

  • Up +1 to 90 w/w
  • Up +0.2% YoY (low -13.6%- high +0.2% this week)

This series has been negative since early 2023. YoY comparisons continued to be negative throughout 2024. It remains frankly recessionary. I suspect this has been a secular change and giving a false signal as a result. Relatively speaking, the YoY reading has been trending “less bad” for the past number of months, and finally, since it is now more than half the way to unchanged on a YoY basis, to neutral as of earlier this year.

Tax Withholding (from the Department of the Treasury)

  • $292.2 B for the month of September this year vs. $277.3 B last year, up +$14.9 B or +5.4%
  • $261.9 B for the last 20 reporting days vs. $259.6 B last year, up +$2.3 B or +0.9%

These were very positive for almost all of 2024, and with the exception of seven weeks (four after an April 15 anomaly), the trend has continued this year. This week it was again mildly positive.

Oil prices and usage (from the E.I.A.)

  • Oil down -$4.63 to $60.69 w/w, down -13.2% YoY ($58.21 - $83.88)
  • Gas prices down -$.05 $3.12 w/w, down -$0.06 YoY
  • Usage 4-week average up +0.5% YoY

(Information can be found at “This Week In Petroleum” at the E.I.A.’s website)

Oil prices first declined into the bottom 1/3rd of their 3 year range, and so turned positive late in 2024, and then declined even further earlier this year. After the Israeli-Iran hostilities last month, oil prices increased, but have since receded back to close to four year lows.

Gas prices also recently made new 3 year lows, and so are also positive. Last year, mileage driven had been generally negative. It turned neutral and then positive earlier this year, but negative again last week. Since March of this year it has oscillated between positive and negative. In general both gas prices and usage have approached neutrality.

Note: given this measure’s extreme volatility, I believe the best measure is against their 3 year average.

Bank lending rates

  • 4.20% Secured Overnight Financing Rate (SOFR), up +0.02 w/w

The TED Spread has been discontinued, and as of last September so was LIBOR. At the suggestion of a reader, I began to track the SOFR instead. Unfortunately, SOFR has only been in existence since 2018, so there is no track record as to how it might behave around normal recessions (vs. the pandemic). Over the past 5 years, it does appear to have matched the trend in LIBOR. Given the alternatives, I am now including it in my list of indicators in the conclusion.

Coincident indicators

Tariff payments (from the Department of the Treasury)

  • August 2025 vs. August 2024: $31.4 B vs. $8.9 B (+$22.5 B)
  • September 2025 vs. September 2024: $31.7 B vs. $8.8 B (+$22.9 B)

I have inaugurated this measure temporarily since other sources, such as inferences from the CPI and PPI, are imprecise and speculative so far. The above information comes directly from the Daily Treasury Statement. These fees are either absorbed by the importer or passed on to the consumer, or some proportion of each, and are removed from potential consumption on other items. Note that some tariff hikes that went into effect last month.

St. Louis FRED Weekly Economic Index

  • Up +0.35 to 2.40 w/w (Low 1.65 November 2, 2024 - high 3.21 November 23, 2024)

This measure was in the neutral range during most of early 2024, but generally positive (above 2.00) since then, and came very close to setting a new 12 month high three months ago, before declining. After four weeks back in neutral range, including a new 6 month low, early this year it returned to positive territory and has remained there since.

Restaurant reservations YoY (from Open Table) State of the Restaurant Industry |

  • Last week - 7 day average up +11% YoY, vs. +6% one week prior

Since 2023 this index has been very volatile, swinging between positive, neutral, and negative several times. In general earlier this year the YoY trends improved, but with a great deal of noise. In summer this metric trended sharply higher, before backing off somewhat in the previous five weeks, and improving this week.

Consumer spending

  • Johnson Redbook up +5.9% YoY, 4 week average +6.1% (high 7.4% 4/25/25; 4.8% 2/1 and 7/3, 2025) United States Redbook Index

The Redbook index has been positive since February of last year. It had very good readings in April and early May, probably due to consumers front-running anticipated tariff price increases and shortages. Afterward it did weaken of a month, but four weeks ago it increased sharply, and has remained very positive since. This presumably means that the period of backlash from previous front-running of the tariffs has ended. I suspect that the “wealth effect” from the sharp increase in stock prices is crucial to this trend. In the past three weeks it has backed off somewhat but still remains very positive.

Consumer inflation by Truflation (Independent, economic & financial data in real time on-chain | Truflation)

  • Up +0.21% to +2.25 YoY (High 3.11% 12/11/24 - Low 1.20% 4/8/25)

This recent addition is a daily update to inflation, similar to the “billion prices project” of the last decade (which required a subscription). I have not added this to my list below of coincident or leading indicators, but needless to say it is an up-to-the-moment reading on this very important indicator. This series has been volatile, and the YoY comparisons had been turning higher since September, but has been very low in the last several months, although it did make another new two month high four months ago.

Real Consumer Spending

  • Up +3.7% YoY (high - 6.0% 4/25/25; 12 month low 1.7% 12/16/24)

This metric premiered at the beginning of last year. One of my most important mantras is that consumption leads employment. Real retail sales have a long history of doing so, but are only reported on a monthly basis.

The weekly result is derived simply by subtracting YoY inflation as measured by Truflation from the YoY change in nominal consumer spending as measured by Redbook. While it will be somewhat noisy, it should anticipate changes in the monthly measures ahead of time. It backed off from the 12 month high it set in February, and then gradually rebounded, and made another 12 month high several months ago. After mainly weeks in the upper part of its 12 month range, it was tepid at the beginning of the year, before improving in the past two months, even making a new 12 month high before backing off. It has improved considerably along with nominal consumer spending in the past several months.

Transport

Railroads (from the AAR)

  • Carloads up +0.9% YoY (cumulative 2025 vs. 2024 up +2.1%)
  • Intermodal units up +1.1% YoY (cumulative up +3.5%)
  • Total loads up +1.0% YoY (cumulative up +2.9%)

(Graph at Railfax Report - North American Rail Freight Traffic Carloading Report )

Shipping transport

  • Harpex down -9 to 2203
  • Baltic Dry Index down -286 to 1980

Rail was mixed for many weeks before turning positive in March, presumably reflecting businesses attempting to get inventory before the new tariffs kick in. Intermodal rail loads had steadily decelerated since then, and five weeks ago turned negative. The total number of total loads was negative for several weeks as well. All three measures improved sharply in July, and remained positive, but weakened in August, and are negative again last week. This week it rebounded to positive again.

Harpex was as high as nearly 4500 almost 2 years ago, but declined as low as 810 in December of last year before rising, setting repeated 12 month highs in the past four months. The usual interpretation for this is that demand is straining against supply, showing a strong global economy, and thus very positive.

Similarly, the BDI was in a generally declining trend throughout 2022 before bottoming at a three year low of 530 in February 2023. The overall trend has been higher since then, rising to a high of 3346 last December, probably reflecting the Houthi attacks on shipping in the Red Sea. This year its low was 1309 in January, and its recent high was 2419 last March. It declined to the bottom 1/3rd of its one year range during the rest of the year, but rebounded all the way to the top of its six month range earlier this year, before retreating back into the middle of that range.

I am wary of reading too much into price indexes like this, since they are heavily influenced by supply (as in, a huge overbuilding of ships in the last decade) as well as demand.

Steel production (American Iron and Steel Institute)

  • Up +0.9% w/w
  • Up +6.8% YoY

This metric has been very volatile for over a year. Recently it improved to neutral and then positive for two weeks before backing off to neutral again in May. Last week its YoY measure was sufficiently small to change the rating to neutral, and eight weeks ago it returned to positive.

Summary And Conclusion

Below are this week’s spreadsheets of the long leading, short leading, and coincident readings. Check marks indicate the present reading. If there has been a change this week, the prior reading is marked with an X:

Long leading Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Corporate bonds ✓
10 year Treasury ✓
10 yr-2 yr Treasury ✓
10 yr. - 3 mo. Treasury x ✓
2 yr - Fed funds ✓
Mortgage rates ✓
Purchase Mtg. Apps. ✓
Refi Mtg Apps. X ✓
Real Estate Loans ✓
Real M1 ✓
Real M2 ✓
Corporate Profits ✓
Adj. Fin. Conditions Index ✓
Leverage Index ✓
Totals: 6 3 5
Short Leading Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Credit Spread ✓
Miller Score ✓
St. L. Fin. Stress Index ✓
US$ Broad ✓
US$ Major currencies ✓
Total commodities ✓
Industrial commodities ✓
Stock prices ✓
Regional Fed New Orders ✓
Initial jobless claims N/a
Temporary staffing ✓
Gas prices ✓
Oil prices ✓
Gas Usage ✓
Totals: 7 4 2
Coincident Indicators Positive Neutral Negative
Weekly Econ. Index ✓
Open Table ✓
Redbook ✓
Rail ✓ X
Harpex ✓
BDI ✓
Steel ✓
Tax Withholding ✓
TED & LIBOR (deleted)
SOFR ✓
Financial Cond. Index ✓
Totals: 8 2 0

.

As per the title this week, one of the advantages of the high frequency indicators I track in this article is that they are not dependent on the functioning of federal agencies. Only one indicator - jobless claims - was unavailable; and if the shutdown drags on I can probably post an estimate based on the States’ individual reports with a one week delay, as the first of those was only posted Friday afternoon.

Let me also reiterate that the commodity prices suggest, contrary to the normal take, that the global economy is weak, but the US$ is even weaker.

Turning to the long leading indicators, the story continues to be that yield curve spreads in the long leading indicators continue to be more positive except for the short end comparisons. Corporate profit growth is also a plus. Meanwhile, housing, on the other hand, is frankly recessionary.

The mechanistic readings of both short leading and coincident indicators are that they are very positive. But the regional Fed services surveys turning negative is a very big caution, mirroring the poor ISM services report on Friday. I continue to believe that consumer spending (which may be being fueled by a wealth effect from stock market gains) remains key to the near term forecast.

data.title: Meta: I'm Buying This Stock On Strong Ad Impressions (Rating Upgrade)
data.description:
For the most part, the majority of gains in the S&P 500 this year have been driven by the small handful of stocks at the very top end of the stock market. Meta Platforms (NASDAQ:META), the sprawling parent company behind Facebook and Instagram, has been one of these stocks, enjoying a ~20% gain since the start of the year. But the core question for investors heading into the last quarter of the year is: which stocks should we still hold onto?

I last wrote a sell article on Meta (META) in May, when the stock was trading near $630 per share. I'll cut to the chase here: my bearishness was short sighted, and I didn't anticipate the very solid results that Meta was able to deliver in Q2. My concern over the company's capex spending plus valuation, while still weighing on the back of my mind, have offsets in strong ad performance and the company's growing chops and clear dominance in AI investments. Rather than miss out on further gains, I'm throwing in the towel on my bear call and upgrading the stock to a buy.

Two major factors have changed since my sell rating on Meta that make me more positive on the stock's prospects. The first is Meta's own performance. To a large extent, nobody can be faulted for thinking that Meta and its apps are saturated, with the combination of Facebook, Instagram, and WhatsApp already covering roughly half of the global population. And yet usage of these apps continues to grow: ad impressions accelerated sharply in Q2, most notably in the U.S., which drives the majority of Meta's revenue (more details in the next section). The healthy performance of Meta's ad platform and its still-rich margin profile are helping to fund the company's (very expansive and costly) ambitions in AI.

Second; while Meta remains trading at a premium valuation, the truth is that the rest of the market is also expensive. Valuation is and always has been relative. At this juncture, greater stock market participation globally in U.S. equity markets, plus crowding into the tech sector, has made many of Meta's large-cap peers more expensive.

Currently, Wall Street analysts are expecting Meta to generate $30.05 in pro forma EPS in FY26, or 6% earnings growth, on 16% revenue growth. This positions Meta's current valuation at 24.2x FY26 P/E.

This is now much cheaper than many large cap tech peers that are at a >30x multiple, as shown above. The only stock cheaper than Meta currently is Alphabet (GOOG) - and arguably, Google is the company that is facing the most pressure from tools like ChatGPT. Google's own AI-powered summaries are leading to a decline in clicks, which may be reducing its ad impressions

We note as well that Meta's $18.2 billion net cash ($47.1 billion of cash, offset against $28.8 billion of debt) gives the company a notable cash cushion (though plenty is earmarked for capex), and pushes down its ex-cash P/E multiple slightly to 24.0x.

In my view, Meta is among the best large-cap tech stocks to invest in at this juncture. Though I recommend rotating the majority of your portfolio out of large cap tech and into cheaper small- and mid-cap stocks, this is the stock to hold onto in that category. Stay long here and keep riding Meta for further upside.

Q2 download

Let's now go through Meta's latest quarterly results in greater detail. The Q2 earnings summary is shown below:

Meta's revenue in Q2 grew at a stunning 22% y/y clip to $47.52 billion, which quashed Wall Street's expectations of $44.84 billion (+15% y/y) by a wide seven point margin.

The company has driven sustained growth by leaning on both user/engagement expansion while still riding higher ad rates. The chart below showcases the company's "daily active people" growing 6% to 3.48 billion, adding 50 million net new daily actives in the quarter. Again, we note here that Meta's ability to keep expanding daily actives will be relatively limited, given its coverage of a substantial portion of the global population. The only major catalysts left are if, for example, China lifts its bans on Meta's apps and allows citizens to access them without using a VPN, which is a barrier to some (but not all) of the world's second most populous nation.

In spite of this, however, we are pleasantly surprised at Meta's ability to accelerate ad impressions; which is a signal that average usage of the Meta family of apps is growing - screen addiction is a global problem, and it's to Meta's benefit. Ad impressions grew 11% y/y globally and accelerated by more than 2x relative to 5% growth in Q1. We're also pleased that this acceleration was buoyed by the U.S. and Canada, where 9% impressions growth also more than doubled versus 4% growth in Q1. This is by far Meta's most monetized region, at 43% of Q2 revenue.

On top of growth in impressions, average prices per ad also grew 9% y/y, with developed markets like North America leading with 11% and 17% growth, respectively. This is an impressive signal of robust advertising appetite despite a waning macroeconomy and weaker consumer spending data.

Though much of the recent chatter about AI is in its generative and automation capabilities, we note that Meta has also been deploying AI to improve the rote mechanics of ad targeting. This was one of the biggest drivers of ad revenue growth in Q2, and why Meta still remains one of the choice platforms for brands to advertise on. Per Mark Zuckerberg's remarks on the Q2 earnings call:

On advertising, the strong performance this quarter is largely thanks to AI unlocking greater efficiency and gains across our ad system. This quarter, we expanded our new AI-powered recommendation model for ads to new surfaces and improved its performance by using more signals and longer context. It's driven roughly 5% more ad conversions on Instagram and 3% on Facebook.

We're also seeing good progress with AI for ad creative with a meaningful percent of our ad revenue now coming from campaigns using one of our generative AI features."

We think as well that with Meta plugged into so much personal data (though privacy is certainly a concern for many), there is room for Meta to create a true ChatGPT competitor with the ability to tap into more personalized context.

On the profitability front, we note Meta's operating income surged 38% y/y to a staggering $20.44 billion. The pace of the company's profit growth is quite a substantial justifier for a mere low-20s P/E multiple. Operating margins soared 5 points y/y to 43%. We do note, however, that the company's burst of capex spending isn't yet fully amortized into the company's profitability and margins.

Pro forma EPS of $7.14 also grew 38% y/y and beat Street expectations of $5.86 with considerable 22% upside.

Risks and key takeaways

There's one more positive point to mention: Meta just announced a new subscription-based, ad-free plan in the UK for £2.99/month (on web) or £3.99/month (on iOS or Android, to compensate for platform fees). This adds on top of an EU subscription already available, and now set to a lower price of €5.99/€7.99. Though these plans is mostly in response to European data privacy laws, I think an ad-free subscription represents an interesting new monetization path for Meta globally. If priced right, this could be net accretive to Meta's earnings if introduced in the U.S. and other major markets, and help reduce Meta's reliance on ad revenue.

Of course, there are risks that we should keep in mind. I continue to worry about Meta's mind-boggling amount of capex, with guidance set at $66-$72 billion for FY25: in other words, nearly as large as the company's TTM operating income. At some point, investors may care about the big bill that AI investments are costing. We note as well that Meta's size and dominance in social media will likely always make it a target for antitrust regulators in the U.S. and across the world.

That being said, given Meta's superb growth execution plus its cheap valuation vis-a-vis other Mag 7 peers, I think the stock has further room to rise. Stay long here.

data.title: Many Large U.S. Insurers Trade In The Green As Shutdown Continues
data.description:
Share of only a handful of large-cap US insurers finished the week in negative territory, while the majority of insurers were either flat or saw gains as Congress failed to reach an agreement to keep the federal government open.

The S&P 500 was up about 1.1% for the week as of close of business Oct. 2 as the broader market seemed to brush off concerns associated with the shutdown. Meanwhile, the US BMI Insurance Index was up 0.2%. The US BMI Banking Index was down 3.2%.

Analysts widely agree that insurance companies are not heavily impacted by a government shutdown. Piper Sandler analyst Paul Newsome said in an interview that insurers are largely able to run their day-to-day business without needing much of anything from the government.

"The states regulate insurers, so even the regulatory process is unaffected," Newsome said. "I suppose if you wanted to avoid the stocks of companies affected negatively by the federal government shutdown, you would find the insurance stocks relatively attractive."

Company performance

Of the large-cap US insurers, many of the top performers this week were managed care companies.

Elevance Health Inc. (ELV), Centene Corp. (CNC), The Cigna Group (CI) and UnitedHealth Group Inc. (UNH) found a place near the top of the group. Elevance saw its stock tick up 6.8% for the week as of close of business Oct. 2. Centene and Cigna shares were up 5.6% and 3.8%, respectively, while UnitedHealth saw gains of 2.8% for the same time period.

The government shutdown has in large part hinged on debate over whether to extend pandemic-era increases to Affordable Care Act tax credits, which are set to expire on Dec. 31. Analysts say, however, that most managed care insurers have already factored for the potential loss of the subsidies as they filed rates with the government for higher premiums.

On the flipside, several life insurance companies were trading down, with Globe Life Inc. (GL) and Prudential Financial Inc. (PRU) finding spots at the bottom of the list of large-cap insurers. Globe Life's stock was down 3.5% for the week as of close of business Oct. 2, and Prudential's shares dropped 2.4%.

Impact of the shutdown

The government's funding lapsed at midnight on Wednesday, Oct. 1, after the House and the Senate failed to pass legislation that would allow the federal workforce to continue to operate and receive payment for services.

Legislators also failed to reauthorize the National Flood Insurance Program (NFIP), which had an expiration date of Sept. 30. CFRA analyst Cathy Seifert noted in an interview that the biggest impact to property and casualty insurers is probably due to the halting of flood policies by the NFIP as new policies can no longer be purchased. Flood policies also are unable to be renewed at this time.

The National Association of Insurance Commissioners (NAIC) sent a group of state regulators to Washington, DC, to meet with members of the Federal Emergency Management Agency Review Council at the White House on Oct. 1, to advocate for long-term reauthorization of the NFIP.

Congress has passed 33 short-term NFIP reauthorizations since 2017, according to the NAIC. The regulatory body also supports a long-term reauthorization of the NFIP that would last for a minimum of 10 years.

If the shutdown extends into the long term, Seifert flagged that there could be ramifications for the overall health of the economy.

"Indirectly, though, a prolonged shutdown could negatively impact business and consumer confidence and ultimately economic activity," Seifert said. "While this slowdown in economic activity could depress demand for commercial insurance, personal lines insurance is relatively economically defensive since it is legally mandated or required in most cases."

Original Post

data.title: Is Silver About To Crash Like In 1980 And 2011?
data.description:
By Jesse Colombo

With silver rallying strongly and rising more than 50% since the start of the year, a growing number of investors are starting to worry that a crash may be coming, similar to what happened after the major spikes in 1980 and 2011, instead of focusing on the incredible long-term opportunity that remains in front of us.

But as one of the few analysts who correctly identified the bull market in both silver and gold from the very beginning, I have consistently urged investors to stop dwelling on negative scenarios and instead recognize the tremendous upside that still lies ahead.

This is silver’s moment to shine, and it is also a moment of vindication for those of us who believed in it all along, even when it was ignored by the mainstream financial world and left for dead as recently as just a few months ago.

I believe silver will not repeat the sharp collapses that followed its short-lived surges in 1980 and 2011. This time is different. This is a legitimate, sustainable bull market with real staying power and the potential for lasting gains.

To start, I want to show you the long-term chart of silver going back six decades to the 1960s. You’ll notice that there have been three major price surges.

The first was the Hunt Brothers–driven spike in 1980, followed by the quantitative easing–fueled rally in 2011, and now the current precious metals bull market. I believe this current move is a legitimate and sustainable bull market that is here to stay, as I will explain throughout this piece.

Now I want you to notice that in both 1980 and 2011, silver surged toward the $50 an ounce level, but that is where those rallies failed and quickly reversed. As a result, the $50 level became a critical price ceiling and resistance level, and it still is to this very day.

For the past two years, as I have been calling for a silver bull market, even when silver was still in the $20 range, I consistently pointed to the $50 level as the key one to watch. I believed it would act like a magnet and draw the price of silver toward it, since major psychological levels often have that effect. And sure enough, silver has steadily climbed toward that level, just as I expected.

Now, with silver trading around $47 to $48 at the time of writing, it is extremely close to breaking through the key $50 level, which is a major make-or-break point.

I also want to point out that many investors bought significant amounts of both silver bullion and ETFs during the frenzy of 2011. Unfortunately, when the price plunged afterward, they were left holding the bag, and many have been sitting on losses for the past 14 years.

But once silver finally surpasses $50, which will mark a new all-time high, no silver investor will be sitting on a loss, at least in nominal terms. That fact alone makes the $50 level a critical psychological barrier that will trigger a surge in investor sentiment once silver breaks above it.

Until recently, many silver investors remained deeply discouraged by years of losses and underperformance. But a decisive break above $50 will mark the beginning of an entirely new era for silver and will set off a powerful virtuous cycle.

Many more investors will pile in, including new and younger participants who never considered silver before and who are not burdened by the baggage carried by older, battle-scarred veterans. This explosion of interest will help drive silver to incredible new heights.

Now I want to say that I adamantly believe silver is going to surpass $50 and go much higher from there in the near future. However, there is something I want to point out. Because silver has surged so strongly and so quickly, it is a bit extended in the short term, which makes it likely that it will consolidate or pause before eventually breaking through $50.

Evidence of this can be seen in the Relative Strength Index (RSI) momentum indicator, shown in the chart below, which provides overbought or oversold readings for assets. I respect what it indicates.

That being said, unlike many amateur investors and analysts, I am adamant that an overbought reading like the current one in silver is not an automatic signal to sell or an indication that the bull market is over.

There is a very high likelihood that silver will experience a shallow pullback or move sideways for a time to work off its overbought condition. This would conserve energy for the next leg higher, when silver smashes through the $50 ceiling.

In April, I called on the very day that gold was likely to consolidate temporarily, but not crash. Sure enough, that is exactly what played out in the following months, setting gold up for the impressive rally it is experiencing today.

I believe a similar scenario is likely to play out in silver. And honestly, I welcome that because I would rather see silver rise in an orderly, more sustainable way than risk burning itself out. Plus, I would like to accumulate more of everything, including silver bullion, mining stocks, and more! And I’m sure you do too.

Now I want to get to the part of this report where I explain why the current bull market in silver is very different from the ones that failed in 1980 and 2011.

The first point I want to make is that while $50 is a key nominal and psychological price ceiling to watch, and I believe silver will break through it decisively, even if it pauses first to work off its overbought condition, it is important to consider how much inflation has occurred over the past several decades.

In real terms, the price of silver remains much lower than those previous peaks. That means $50 in 1980 or 2011 is certainly not the same as $50 in 2025, as anyone who has recently been to the grocery store can easily understand.

To illustrate that point, I created a chart showing the real, or inflation-adjusted, price of silver over the past six decades. On the chart, I highlighted what the $50 silver peaks in 1980 and 2011 would be worth in 2025 dollars. It turns out that in 1980, silver reached the equivalent of $199, and in 2011, it reached the equivalent of $72!

Now compare that to today, with silver trading around $47, which is much lower than either of those past peaks.

For that reason alone, not even including the additional points I will explain next, I believe the current silver bull market is sustainable, has much more room to run, and should be able to finally smash through the $50 ceiling that has held it back for so long.

To further reinforce my point above, I also want to show you the ratio of silver to the U.S. M2 money supply, indexed to 100. This may be an even more accurate measure of inflation than the Consumer Price Index (CPI) used to create the previous chart. After all, the root cause of inflation is growth in the money supply itself. As Milton Friedman, the Nobel Prize–winning economist, famously said, “Inflation is always and everywhere a monetary phenomenon.”

Similar to what the real silver price chart revealed, this chart shows that although silver is approaching the same nominal price of $50, its real-world price is much lower than it was in 1980 and 2011.

For example, in 1980, the ratio was 1,038; in 2011, it was 176; and now it is just 66. I see this as clear evidence that, despite its recent gains, silver’s bull market is nowhere near being long in the tooth. It still has substantial room to rise, and it should be able to blow through the $50 level with ease.

Next, let’s move on to another metric that confirms that silver is still much cheaper today than it was at the peaks in 1980 and 2011, despite its recent gains.

This time, we will look at the silver-to-gold ratio, which is a useful way to determine whether silver is undervalued or overvalued relative to gold, the leading benchmark in the precious metals market.

While gold has always been more expensive than silver throughout history, the gap between them has varied significantly. At the 1980 peak, silver was 6.7% of the price of gold. At the 2011 peak, it was 3.3%. But now, silver is just 1.2% of gold’s price, which is far below historical levels.

This indicates that silver is extremely cheap by historical standards and still has substantial room for its bull market to continue. And based on the duration of past precious metals bull markets, I believe silver still has at least another decade to run, and the same goes for gold.

Another eye-opening metric that confirms silver is still extremely cheap is the ratio of silver to the U.S. federal debt, which now stands at $37.8 trillion and is growing at an alarming pace of $1 trillion every 100 days with no signs of slowing.

This is an important metric because it shows whether the price of silver has kept pace with the growth of the national debt, and the answer is clearly no.

The chart, indexed to 100, shows that this ratio was 1,377 at the 1980 peak, 87 at the 2011 peak, and only 33 today. This indicates that silver has substantial room to catch up to the expanding national debt. In that context, $50 silver is not expensive by any measure, and a move to $100 or more is far from inconceivable.

Another reason why this ratio is so important is that the higher the federal debt rises relative to GDP, the closer we move to the inevitable breaking point where the government and the Federal Reserve will be forced to support the U.S. Treasury market and fund government operations by running the printing presses on overdrive.

This will send inflation through the roof and ultimately destroy the dollar, causing gold and silver to reach prices that are difficult to even comprehend.

This will not be ordinary inflation, but full-blown hyperinflation like what my great-grandparents suffered through in Weimar Germany during the 1920s. It devastated the wealth and economy of Germany and was the reason they emigrated to the United States. Unfortunately, it also led to mass radicalization, which directly paved the way for the rise of Hitler.

Unfortunately, this debt problem is truly a worldwide phenomenon, as global debt has surged more than tenfold since the mid-1990s, reaching an estimated $250 trillion. This towering debt burden is a ticking time bomb that will ultimately bring fiat currencies to their knees.

That is why it is of the utmost importance for everyone to acquire at least some physical gold and silver to protect themselves against what lies ahead. This fact alone guarantees that precious metals still have much further to rise.

Another useful way to determine whether silver is cheap or expensive compared to its past is to measure it against another yardstick: the Dow Jones Industrial Average.

This comparison is valuable because there is a long-established relationship between precious metals and stocks, with capital rotating back and forth between them in secular phases. In many ways, they act as counterbalances to each other.

The chart of the silver-to-Dow ratio, indexed to 100, shows that silver at its 1980 peak reached an astounding 3,939. At the 2011 peak, it was 282. Today, it is only 68. This confirms that silver remains very cheap and that $50 is no longer a particularly high price.

The reason for this extremely low ratio is twofold: the U.S. stock market is highly inflated and expensive right now, while silver remains undervalued by nearly every metric.

I believe this situation will reverse, with silver far outperforming as stocks decline and their lofty valuations return to more realistic levels. That reversal will benefit precious metals enormously, as trillions of dollars flow out of a sinking stock market and into a booming silver and gold market, sending them dramatically higher.

As we wrap up this report, I want to leave you on an extremely exciting note. Silver has been forming a powerful chart pattern known as a cup and handle for the past six decades. This pattern indicates that once silver breaks out, it will almost certainly soar to at least several hundred dollars an ounce.

I believe a reasonable target is between $300 and $500, and that does not even factor in the inevitability of hyperinflation, which will send silver to prices measured not just in hundreds, but in billions and even trillions of dollars per ounce.

So this brings us back to the critical $50 threshold that I have discussed throughout this report.

In order to confirm the cup and handle pattern and the extremely bullish scenario, silver must decisively close above that level.

As I said earlier, that will be a game changer for investor sentiment toward silver, because once it happens, nobody who has ever bought it will be holding at a loss.

The story will shift from years of frustration to stories of incredible fortunes made, the kind of life-changing, generational wealth that investors dream of. I am excited and waiting for that breakout, but silver may pause for a period of time to catch its breath first, and I actually hope it does because I want to make a few more purchases before it takes off for good.

So in conclusion, I have made the case throughout this report that while silver’s rallies in 1980 and 2011 were short-lived spikes that ended in spectacular crashes and left investors discouraged for decades, the current move is very different.

This time, silver is in a sustainable and organic bull market in which prices will rise significantly and remain at those high levels permanently.

One of the key reasons that outcome will happen is because silver today is much cheaper than it was in 1980 and 2011 when measured against multiple benchmarks, including inflation, the money supply, gold, the U.S. national debt, and the stock market.

These are extremely exciting and promising times for precious metals investors, and I am grateful to have you on this journey with me. I hope you prosper greatly in the years ahead.

data.title: Undercovered Dozen: Toast, Archer Aviation, Klaviyo And More
data.description:
The Undercovered Dozen is a weekly Seeking Alpha editor-curated series highlighting 12 articles on lesser-covered stocks from the previous seven days. We hope this provides ideas and inspires discussion among the community.

Today, we're looking at articles published between Sep. 26 and Oct. 2.

Take a look at what these less-covered stocks might hold for you. And please join the conversation below to share what you think: Are any of these worth following up on? And are there other undercovered ideas that you like?

Klaviyo Is A Sleeping Giant (Strong Buy Initiation)

PropNotes | Strong Buy

Klaviyo (NYSE:KVYO) is a stock that came across our radar recently. It’s a B2B SaaS company that provides a B2C CRM product for businesses. CRM, for those who don’t know, stands for 'customer relationship management.' Historically, CRM software has been geared towards the B2B market, as sales teams have needed a way to keep track of their deals and prospects.

Now, however, as we advance into an increasingly digital age, having one central place where a business can market, sell, and service retail customers makes a lot of sense. With one integrated platform that maps directly to three of the seven key business functions, KVYO's platform has an incredibly strong value proposition.

In recent years, the company has performed spectacularly, growing revenues like clockwork while maintaining robust gross margins. And yet, since the IPO in 2023, shares have traded down.

We don’t think this makes a lot of sense.

Today, we'll dive deeper into KVYO's business, highlight the valuation, and make the case that this B2B SaaS firm belongs in your portfolio.

Read more here...

Palo Alto Networks $19B Surprise Few See Coming

Uttam Dey | Buy

Early last month, cybersecurity heavyweight Palo Alto Networks (NASDAQ:PANW) crashed ~18% after announcing its largest acquisition ever—a $25B takeover of cybersecurity access security firm CyberArk (NASDAQ:CYBR).

The acquisition announcement by the cybersecurity giant from Palo Alto, CA, initially received flak with some analysts citing the lack of “meaningful synergies” in the acquisition and downgraded Palo Alto Networks shares or PANW.

At the time, I was already in the process of securing some exposure towards PANW’s shares in my portfolio and the 18% drop in its shares looked like an adverse over-reaction to the acquisition news.

My thesis for buying PANW’s shares was simple—that it was “perfectly positioned to enter new growth markets,” as I noted in my previous research, and markets were, simultaneously discounting PANW’s ability to keep up its +35% FCF margins and its industry-leading revenue run rates, supported by backlog that is expected to swell to $19B next year.

PANW’s updates from the past 30-60 days have further deepened my conviction in its shares, and I continue to recommend a Buy on its shares.

Read more here...

W. P. Carey: Separation From The Others Is Just Beginning

Julian Lin | Buy

W. P. Carey (NYSE:WPC) is finally starting to trade at a premium to its historical comparable peers, but that’s by intention. The company has successfully transformed its portfolio to focus on industrial and warehouse properties, which have superior economics and resiliency compared to the traditional retail or office model. While capital recycling remains elevated due to the high interest rate environment, the company is still positioned for consistent and persistent growth. I see continued upside ahead, with investors enjoying a generous dividend yield as they wait. I reiterate my buy rating for the stock.

Read more here...

WPAY: A Weekly Paying Alternative To YMAX

Cain Lee | Buy

This is easily one of the best times in modern history to be an income investor. There is such an abundance of different funds that now pay distributions on a weekly basis and as time goes on, the funds have become more efficient in terms of stability. Roundhill WeeklyPay Universe ETF (BATS:WPAY) is the latest addition to my portfolio because I believe it has the potential to be the top performing weekly paying option ETF in the market. WPAY launched September 3rd, 2025 and is managed by Roundhill Investments. The fund has a very reasonable 0.99% expense ratio, considering that investors are getting a huge distribution yield in return.

Read more here...

MercadoLibre: Two Times Three Is $100 Billion

RB Equity | Strong Buy

MELI is the most valuable company in LatAm despite also being one of the youngest listed entities in the region, only turning 26 this year.

How is that possible?

LatAm is a region with over +600 million people (about ~2x US). With the three largest economies being Brazil (211 million, GDP $2.3 Trillion), Mexico (130 million, GDP $1.9 Trillion) and Argentina (45 million, GDP $650 Billion). The big three thus contain about 2/3 of non-English-speaking population in the continent and more than that in GDP share.

Holding a dominant position in the "LatAm big three" is a good first step to becoming the largest corporation. MELI did this already. The second step is to be in an industry with a very long runway for growth (and hence command premium multiples). MELI is there as well, not with one but with both of its businesses.

Read more here...

Archer Aviation: Takeoff Is Imminent

Bay Area Ideas | Buy

Air mobility is one of the next frontiers of advancement in transportation. The U.S. and China are in a race to deploy such technologies, but in late 2024, I decided that Archer Aviation Inc. (NYSE:ACHR) stock wasn't ready for takeoff yet. I initiated with a Sell rating, and since then Archer has slightly underperformed the S&P 500 (SP500). I believe giving an update now would be appropriate, as there have been many new developments for the company.

In the below analysis, I discover that the growth story is highly intact and many developments now show commercialization is right around the corner. While the company did dilute shareholders to strengthen their cash position, dilution risk is likely behind us for the near term. Furthermore, Archer's P/B ratio has contracted significantly from all-time highs, and so this is a great opportunity for investors. As a result, I have decided to double upgrade the stock to a buy rating.

Read more here...

GE Vernova: Higher For Longer Growth Rate

Ricardo Fernandez | Buy

One of my key sector focuses has been the broader electricity energy ecosystem, encompassing fuels (natural gas and uranium), independent power producers, construction companies, cable suppliers, and power plant or gas turbine manufacturers. All have done well as US load demand rises, driven by climate change, electrification, and data center demand. I began covering GE Vernova (NYSE:GEV) (NEOE:GEV:CA) in June 2024, citing the turnaround that was developing, and then followed up with a view on higher multiple potential. Now I update my coverage and maintain a buy on higher for longer growth.

Read more here...

Aya Gold & Silver: Free Cash Flow In Sight With Dazzlingly High Silver Prices

Alberto Abaterusso | Buy

This article recommends a BUY rating for Aya Gold & Silver Inc. (TSX:AYA:CA), a stock listed on the TSX exchange. This represents an upside from our previous analysis, which issued a hold rating on October 6, 2024. ...

AYA's shares, listed on the TSX (“Toronto Stock Exchange”), have fallen by -28.44% compared to the share price at the time of our previous analysis's Hold rating and have underperformed the S&P 500 (change +15.11%). Returns are as of this new article on AYA shares. The S&P 500 is the benchmark for the US stock market. In principle, AYA stock is an excellent long-term investment, as its performance on the OTCQX speaks volumes: if you compare this stock to its direct competitors over the long term, it is very likely to outperform most of them significantly.

Read more here...

Fiserv: A Value Investor's Golden Ticket

Arjun Pathy | Strong Buy

Management guidance cuts, product rollout issues, and a poorly timed CEO transition have spooked Fiserv (NYSE:FI) investors, resulting in a more than 40% YTD underperformance compared to fintech benchmark ETF FINX. Out of this overblown reaction, Fiserv has emerged as a cheap value play built on strong expansion in margin and revenues, an efficient capital structure, and a strategy of aggressively returning cash to shareholders. Going into Q2 earnings, FI traded at nearly 30x earnings, and despite an EPS beat, after management set annual revenue guidance at 10% (the low end of a previous 10-12% guidance), the stock fell to a 52-week low.

Sharp competition from Toast and Stripe, four concurrent acquisitions, and rollout delays across XD, Cash Flow Central, and Clover’s healthcare expansion compounded concerns. Such advancements would typically prompt a minor correction, but instead led to a dramatic retreat from all-time highs. Contrary to popular sentiment, strong fundamentals, especially when put into context with competitors, indicate that entering FI is a bargain at current prices.

Read more here...

Lineage: A Deeply Discounted Cold Storage Giant Positioned To Consolidate The Industry

Michael Duke | Buy

Lineage (NASDAQ:LINE) is the largest cold storage operator in the world. Like its closest peer, Americold (COLD), it owns and operates temperature-controlled warehouses that serve as mission-critical nodes in the global supply chain for food, pharmaceuticals, and other perishables. The business model combines long-lived infrastructure with active logistics operations, producing a unique REIT-like hybrid. But while Lineage has global scale and strategic importance, its stock has fallen over 50% from recent highs.

Despite this drawdown, Lineage remains a dominant force in the cold chain and may be even better positioned than Americold to benefit from the long-term growth and consolidation of the industry. The business faces real challenges, oversupply, cost inflation, and softening occupancy, but the stock now trades at a valuation that prices in these risks and leaves room for upside if the industry normalizes.

Read more here...

Bloom Energy: The Not Too Overvalued Bet Fueling The Data Center CapEx Boom

Sarfatti Investment Research | Strong Buy

I am initiating coverage on Bloom Energy (NYSE:BE) with a Strong Buy rating. BE is very well positioned in today’s market to deploy its fuel cell solutions to support the rapid data center CapEx expansion fueled by AI. Further exacerbating BE’s growth prospects is the current natural gas-forward energy mix in the US, with little propensity to change in the medium term. My thesis will be focused largely on data centers, as this is where growth is concentrated, with a brief aside on maritime developments.

Read more here...

Toast Won't Remain Cheap For Too Long

Amrita Roy | Buy

When I last wrote about Toast (NYSE:TOST), I upgraded the stock to a “buy,” as the company continued to see momentum in its core US market while closing its largest enterprise deal with Applebee’s, demonstrating the versatility of its verticalized product strategy.

While I have frequently pointed out that a slowing economy will put pressure on Toast, what amazed me was that the management raised their full-year forward guidance while continuing to operate with superior financial discipline.

Unfortunately the stock has dropped quite considerably since August and saw another round of downward volatility this past week as news emerged of the company reducing pricing for its software packages aimed at smaller restaurants.

Since then, the pricing has reverted back to its original levels, while its Q2 FY25 earnings continued to show rapid progress in Toast’s initiatives in expanding its TAM (total addressable market) across enterprise, retail, and international markets.

What was also optimistic was that management raised its full-year guidance even higher, defying concerns of macroeconomic pressures.

I believe the recent 25% drawdown in the stock price since August has created an extraordinary opportunity in the stock with a significantly higher risk-reward than before. I am reiterating my price target at $55.5 per share, which translates to a potential upside of 49% from its current levels, making it a “buy”.
